title: Comfort Measures in Labor: Nonpharmacologic Support
subtitle: A 26-year-old in active labor with back pain wants to cope without medication for now. The nurse uses position changes, hydrotherapy, counterpressure, breathing, and continuous support to ease discomfort and aid labor.

patient.name: Aisha Bello
patient.admitDate: Labor and delivery, G1 in active labor with contractions every 3 minutes and reassuring fetal heart tones. Reports back pain (suspected occiput-posterior). Prefers nonpharmacologic comfort measures for now.

nursesNote: Aisha Bello, 26, G1 in active labor (contractions q3min, reassuring FHR) with back pain from a suspected occiput-posterior position; prefers nonpharmacologic comfort for now. Comfort measures: frequent position changes and upright positions/ambulation; side-lying to avoid supine hypotension; hydrotherapy (warm shower/tub); sacral counterpressure for back labor; coached paced breathing and relaxation; massage; a calm environment with continuous labor support (partner/doula reduces interventions); and regular bladder emptying (a full bladder impedes progress and comfort). These complement — not replace — pharmacologic options (epidural, IV opioid, nitrous) the patient may choose.
providerOrders: Provide nonpharmacologic comfort measures per the patient's preferences
Encourage frequent position changes, upright positions, and ambulation as appropriate
Offer hydrotherapy, counterpressure, massage, and breathing/relaxation coaching
Encourage regular bladder emptying; avoid prolonged supine positioning
Offer pharmacologic options (epidural/IV/nitrous) as desired; provide continuous support

opts.text: Nonpharmacologic measures can improve comfort and coping in labor
Position changes and upright positions can aid comfort and labor progress
Hydrotherapy (warm shower or tub) is a comfort option in labor
Counterpressure helps with back labor
The laboring woman should remain flat on her back for comfort
Continuous labor support reduces the need for interventions
Side-lying or upright positions help avoid supine hypotension
Breathing and relaxation techniques help manage contraction pain
A full bladder can impede labor and comfort — encourage voiding
Nonpharmacologic measures complement, not replace, pharmacologic options
Lying supine for long periods is the best position in labor
A calm environment and a support person enhance coping
Assess the woman's pain, coping, and preferences
Offer position changes, upright positions, and ambulation
Add hydrotherapy, counterpressure, and breathing/relaxation
Encourage bladder emptying and continuous support
Reassess comfort and offer pharmacologic options as desired; document
Encourage position changes and upright positions/ambulation
Offer a warm shower or tub (hydrotherapy)
Apply sacral counterpressure for back labor
Coach paced breathing and relaxation
Provide continuous support and a calm environment
Encourage regular bladder emptying
Keep the laboring woman flat on her back for long periods
Restrict her to bed with no position changes
Leave her alone without support during contractions

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess pain and coping and ask her preferences
Help her into comfortable upright/side-lying positions and ambulate
Provide hydrotherapy, counterpressure, and coached breathing
Encourage voiding and involve her support person
Reassess, offer additional options, and document
She copes well with breathing and position changes
Hydrotherapy and counterpressure ease her back pain
Continuous support helps her stay calm and in control
She empties her bladder and labor progresses comfortably
Lying flat causes dizziness and a drop in blood pressure
A full bladder slows progress and increases discomfort
Left alone and anxious, her pain and tension increase
Immobilized in bed, she becomes more uncomfortable
